inbound.subject: Starting over after a layoff
inbound.body: Hi coach, I want to get back to running after a messy few months. My big goal is to get healthy enough to race a half marathon again this spring. Right now I mostly need help rebuilding without doing something stupid.

outbound.subject: Re: Starting over after a layoff
outbound.text:
Thanks — I’m glad you reached out and I can help you rebuild safely toward a spring half.
Before I write a conservative, low-risk rebuild plan I need a few quick details so the plan matches your time, current status, and risk. Please answer the short questions below and I’ll follow with a clear first microcycle you can start next week.
1) Target: do you have a specific half-marathon date or an approximate month this spring?
2) Time availability: how many days per week can you realistically run, and how much time (minutes) do you have for your longest weekend session?
3) Current training: what have you been doing the last 4 weeks (running frequency and longest run)?
4) Injury/health: any current pain or injuries? If yes, where, when they started, whether a clinician cleared you to run, and whether symptoms worsen during/after running.
5) Constraints/preferences: any days/times you can’t train, preferred surfaces (treadmill/road/trail), and is cross‑training (bike/elliptical/strength) acceptable?
6) Anything else you’re worried about or I should know (sleep, work travel, prior injuries that still bug you)?
7) Would you prefer a quick 15‑minute call instead of answering by email? If yes, share 2–3 windows that work.
Once you reply I’ll draft the conservative Week 1 microcycle for your initial_assessment block.
outbound.html:
Thanks — I’m glad you reached out and I can help you rebuild safely toward a spring half.

Before I write a conservative, low-risk rebuild plan I need a few quick details so the plan matches your time, current status, and risk. Please answer the short questions below and I’ll follow with a clear first microcycle you can start next week.

1) Target: do you have a specific half-marathon date or an approximate month this spring?
2) Time availability: how many days per week can you realistically run, and how much time (minutes) do you have for your longest weekend session?
3) Current training: what have you been doing the last 4 weeks (running frequency and longest run)?
4) Injury/health: any current pain or injuries? If yes, where, when they started, whether a clinician cleared you to run, and whether symptoms worsen during/after running.
5) Constraints/preferences: any days/times you can’t train, preferred surfaces (treadmill/road/trail), and is cross‑training (bike/elliptical/strength) acceptable?
6) Anything else you’re worried about or I should know (sleep, work travel, prior injuries that still bug you)?
7) Would you prefer a quick 15‑minute call instead of answering by email? If yes, share 2–3 windows that work.

Once you reply I’ll draft the conservative Week 1 microcycle for your initial_assessment block.

inbound.subject: A little more context
inbound.body: A bit more context: I used to run consistently before the layoff and I'd call myself intermediate. I can train four days a week most weeks if we keep it realistic. The thing that nags me is mild Achilles tightness when I get greedy. I also do better when the plan feels structured.

outbound.subject: Re: A little more context
outbound.text:
Welcome back—great to have you starting this rebuild with clarity and structure.
Thanks for the extra context. With your mild Achilles tightness when loading increases and your preference for a structured plan, I’ll anchor Week 1 to a conservative base that uses four easy-running days per week plus targeted mobility/strength work to support the Achilles. This approach prioritizes consistent, low-risk progression so you can rebuild confidence and run without flare-ups. I’ll draft the concrete Week 1 microcycle for your feedback and keep you on a steady, predictable cadence.
- Week 1 of your initial assessment block: four easy-run days per week at an easy pace
- Light mobility work focused on calves, ankles, and Achilles, plus simple strength work to support the area
- Built-in recovery and a gradual, safe progression to avoid aggravation
- I’ll share the concrete Week 1 microcycle for your approval and any tweaks
Safety note: if you experience sharp pain or increased tightness, back off and adjust the plan.
Goal: rebuild consistency toward a spring half-marathon.

inbound.subject: Profile details and target race
inbound.body: Here is the missing context more directly. Primary goal: run a half marathon on 2026-05-17 and finish feeling strong. Time availability: 4 sessions per week and about 4 to 5 hours total. Experience level: intermediate. Constraints: mild Achilles tightness, busy work mornings on Tuesdays, and I prefer structured guidance.

outbound.subject: Re: Profile details and target race
outbound.text:
Got it — thanks for the clear details. I’ve drafted a conservative Week 2 microcycle that fits 4 sessions/week, avoids Tuesday morning, and keeps load low for your mild Achilles tightness.
Confirmed: half-marathon goal, 4 sessions/week (~4–5 hr availability), mild Achilles tightness — no questions.
Week 2 microcycle (simple, structured, conservative):
- Monday: Easy run 35 min (conversational effort) + 15–20 min strength (single-leg glute bridges, squats or split squats, calf eccentric protocol 3x10 slow lowers).
- Tuesday: Busy morning — no running session scheduled. Evening: 10–12 min calf/ankle mobility (heel drops, ankle circles, calf foam rolling).
- Wednesday: Easy run 40 min (conversational). Keep cadence relaxed; no pickups/strides.
- Thursday: Optional cross-train 30–45 min (easy bike or swim) or full rest depending on how you feel.
- Friday: Easy run 30 min + 15–20 min strength (same template as Monday; add single-leg balance work).
- Saturday: Long easy run 55–65 min (steady conversational pace). Focus on duration, not pace.
- Sunday: Rest or very easy walk + 10 min mobility.
(Approx weekly time ≈ 3.5–4.5 hours depending on cross-train choice.)
Achilles monitoring rules:
- If tightness is mild and improves with warm-up and does not worsen during/after sessions, continue as planned.
- If you feel sharp pain during a session or a marked increase in next-morning pain/stiffness, stop running that day and tell me before the next session.
- Don’t add any speed work, strides, hills, or tempo this week — treat this as an assessment week.
Strength & mobility specifics:
- Eccentric calf (Alfredson-style): 3x10 slow lowers, once daily or at least after one run per day when tolerated.
- 2x/week 15–20 min strength: single-leg glute work, squats/lunges, ankle stability.
- Daily 5–10 min calf/ankle mobility and foam rolling after runs.
- Helpful note: watch a short Alfredson eccentric heel-drop demo video to check technique.
What I need from you after this week: report which sessions you completed and how the Achilles felt during warm-up, during the run, and the next morning. If all sessions complete with only mild/no increase in tightness, we’ll repeat a similar week and then progress one lever (duration) next.
Proceed with this week and send the brief report as described.
outbound.html:

inbound.subject: Availability update for this week
inbound.body: Availability update for this week. Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 4. Pain score: 2 out of 10. Risk candidate feels green. I can run Monday, Wednesday, Friday, and Sunday. Week is not chaotic. Schedule variability is medium because work may move one session by a few hours.

outbound.subject: Re: Availability update for this week
outbound.text:
Four-session week confirmed for Mon/Wed/Fri/Sun in Week 3 of your initial assessment block.
We'll keep to the Week 3 conservative microcycle, with easy runs and Achilles-care as the priority.
Weekly plan:
- Mon: 25–30 min easy run
- Wed: 25–30 min easy run
- Fri: 25–30 min easy run
- Sun: 25–30 min easy run
- Calf/ankle mobility daily
- Two short strength/conditioning blocks
- Alfredson eccentric calf protocol as scheduled
If a session needs shifting by a few hours, that’s fine. After the week, please share how each session felt (warm-up, during, and next-morning soreness) so we can adjust if needed.
outbound.html:
Four-session week confirmed for Mon/Wed/Fri/Sun in Week 3 of your initial assessment block.

We'll keep to the Week 3 conservative microcycle, with easy runs and Achilles-care as the priority.

Weekly plan:

- Wed: 25–30 min easy run
- Fri: 25–30 min easy run
- Sun: 25–30 min easy run
- Calf/ankle mobility daily
- Two short strength/conditioning blocks
- Alfredson eccentric calf protocol as scheduled

If a session needs shifting by a few hours, that’s fine. After the week, please share how each session felt (warm-up, during, and next-morning soreness) so we can adjust if needed.
